projected_target.projected_slide_heading_text: Slide 1. Psychological Disorders in the DSM-5-TR

normalized_workflow.text: Presented By Kathryn Bangs, PhD, LCP, NCSP, PEL, LPHA
Mental disorder: Significant impairment in psychological functioning, such as schizophrenia or major depression
Statistical abnormality: Scoring very high or low on some dimension

non_visual_properties.descr: The x-axis of a chart shows the characteristic level of anxiety. It is divided into 3 parts, Extremely low, Moderate, and Extremely high. The moderate part is shown to extend from low on the left to high on the right. The widths of extremely low and extremely high are the same while the width of moderate is significantly high. The y-axis shows the number of cases. The chart shows a bell curve over the x-axis. It starts from the middle of extremely low part of the x-axis, curves up, reaches its peak at the middle of the moderate part of the x-axis, curves down, and ends at the middle of the extreme high part of the x-axis. The chart shows the note abnormal with a question mark on the extremely low and extremely high parts of the chart.

normalized_workflow.label: Visual 1

normalized_workflow.text: Diagnosis can carry stigma—labels may unintentionally lead others to judge, stereotype, or fear individuals with mental health conditions.
Some people may avoid seeking help because they fear being “labeled” or treated differently after receiving a diagnosis.
Language matters: using person-first terms (e.g., “a person with schizophrenia” rather than “a schizophrenic”) helps reduce bias and promote dignity.
Clinicians must balance accuracy and empathy, recognizing that diagnoses describe symptoms, not identity or worth.
Reducing diagnostic stigma involves promoting understanding that mental health disorders are common, treatable, and exist along a continuum of human experience.

warning_entries.warning_message: Reading-order heuristic may be weak for this slide.

normalized_workflow.text: Environmental: Head injuries or exposure to toxins
Neurocognitive includes impairment in old age caused by deterioration of the brain
Associated with a variety of mental illness
Stress, trauma, learning disorders, and a lack of knowledge, control, or mastery
Blend of environmental and psychosocial stressors as well as inherited vulnerability
Psychotic disorders, mood and personality disorders, and anxiety disorders

non_visual_properties.descr: A chart shows 5 horizontal stacked bars. A vertical line runs to the right of the top 3 bars. The bottom 2 bars cross the vertical line. Each stacked bar shows two bars, vulnerability and stress. The vulnerability bars of the top 2 stacked bars are of the same length. However, the stress bar of the second stacked bar is longer than the first. The total length of the second and third stacked bars is the same. However, the vulnerability bar of the third is longer than that of the second. The total length of the fourth stacked bar is shorter than the total length of the fifth stacked bar. The two stacked bars cross the vertical line. The vulnerability bars of the 2 stacked bars are of the same length as that of the third stacked bar. The stress bar of the fourth stacked bar is shorter than that of the fifth stacked bar. The region of the chart to the right of the vertical line is labeled degree of psychopathology. It is divided vertically into low, medium, and high. The fourth stacked bar extends up to medium. The fifth stacked bar extends up to high.

normalized_workflow.text: Most disorders have symptoms that we all experience
A diagnosis depends on intensity, length of time, and the impact on the person

normalized_workflow.chosen_title: Purpose and Organization of the DSM-5-TR

projected_target.projected_slide_heading_text: Slide 7. Purpose and Organization of the DSM-5-TR

normalized_workflow.text: Purpose and Organization of the DSM-5-TR
Standardized system for diagnosing mental disorders
Over 400 disorders grouped by symptom and developmental similarities
Combination of categorical and dimensional approaches
Provides a common language for describing mental disorders.
Aims to improve diagnostic consistency and treatment planning.
Supports research, education, and communication across professionals.
Promotes a dimensional understanding of mental health.
Top Level: Major diagnostic classes (e.g., Mood, Anxiety, Psychotic).
Next Level: Specific disorders (e.g., Major Depressive Disorder).
Lowest Level: Subtypes, specifiers, and severity ratings.
Specifiers add detail about onset, pattern, or features (e.g., “with psychotic features”).
Severity ratings indicate symptom intensity (e.g., mild, moderate, severe).
These refinements help tailor treatment and prognosis.
Schizophrenia Spectrum and Other Psychotic Disorders

normalized_workflow.chosen_title: Schizophrenia Spectrum and Other Psychotic Disorders

projected_target.projected_slide_heading_text: Slide 12. Schizophrenia Spectrum and Other Psychotic Disorders

normalized_workflow.text: Schizophrenia Spectrum and Other Psychotic Disorders
Delusions: Fixed, false beliefs that are not based in reality and are resistant to evidence or reasoning
Hallucinations: Sensory perceptions that occur without any external stimulus
Disorganized thinking: Disruption in the logical connection between thoughts, often reflected in speech
Negative symptoms: Absence or reduction of normal emotional and behavioral functioning (flat affect, alogia, anhedonia, avolition)

normalized_workflow.text: Bipolar Disorders: Alternating manic and depressive episodes
Mania: is a state of abnormally elevated, expansive, or irritable mood, accompanied by increased energy, activity, and other changes in behavior
Depressive Disorders: Persistent sadness, loss of interest, fatigue, and hopelessness
Triggered by exposure to traumatic events

projected_target.projected_slide_heading_text: Slide 17. Dissociative and Somatic Symptom Disorders

normalized_workflow.text: Disruptions in memory, identity, or consciousness (e.g., Dissociative Identity Disorder)
Physical symptoms without clear medical cause (e.g., Somatic Symptom Disorder, Illness Anxiety Disorder)

projected_target.projected_slide_heading_text: Slide 18. Feeding, Eating, and Elimination Disorders
Slide 20. Disruptive, Impulse-Control, and Conduct Disorders

normalized_workflow.text: Involve problems with emotional and behavioral regulation
Neurocognitive: Decline in memory or cognition
Enduring and inflexible patterns of behavior
Cluster A: marked by odd, eccentric, or suspicious behavior
Cluster B: characterized by dramatic, emotional, or erratic behaviors
Cluster C: involve anxious and fearful behavior

projected_target.projected_slide_heading_text: Slide 24. Personality Disorders vs. Other Disorders
Slide 27. Cluster B Disorders (Dramatic, Emotional, Erratic)
